item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm a bit late to this — I've had this on my to-read list for a while — but I'm choosing to interpret this as an anomalous religious cult from the future using time-travel and Ways to escape the persecution of the futuristic "SCP Federation"? And the emphasis on being before 1972 is because the site was contained in 1972, ergo the suicides.

There's obviously a bit more here but I'm loving the opportunity to analyze this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is solid but a few things are holding me back from upvoting it at the moment. I like the core of this a lot even though I can't quite tell what's going on (me not being able to tell what's going on is not a problem, far from it), but I'm going to back up DeepViolet's concerns about the tone. They take me out of the article at a couple key moments where I feel like you need to really be roping people in, and it colors the rest of the piece for me.

Some places I'd consider re-toning things other than what Violet pointed out:

+ show block
– hide block

It is of unknown construction and design, and is believed to have been built in the 4th millenium BCE, apparently for the worship of an unknown deity named Sap'e'ha.

The gist here is fine but the phrasing "unknown deity named Sap'e'ha" seems odd to me. If they know the name, then it's not really unknown, is it? I might amend this to "previously unattested" or similar.

In cases where burn wounds occur, any clothing and items the SCP-4858-A manifestation has on its person will ordinarily be destroyed, although there are a small number of cases where objects have survived. The causes of this are unknown.

This entire bit just feels clunky to me. I feel like there are several ways you could rephrase this to make it flow a bit better. I wish I could be a bit more specific here but I've now spent a good ten minutes trying to think of a way to make this same content flow more naturally and I can't. Still, I think there must be one.

I must clarify that ultimately these are minor complaints, I still quite like the majority of the article and I feel like it really picks up during the inscription list and recovery log. (Incidentally, I might advise noting the language that the written notes are written in, since you do that for the inscriptions.)

But yeah, I feel like these comparatively minor problems being fixed could take it from a decent article to a very good one, and I'm withholding a vote for now on that basis.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

If Violet and I are both completely out in the weeds and the phrasing is an intentional stylistic choice, I'd be curious to know why, if you can divulge it without overexplaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is the outline of an outstanding mystery here, but I can't piece together the bigger picture. Not without trying, though: the tidbits of information sprinkled throughout are more than enough to satisfy me. +1, this is a really intriguing and almost magical piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was fun! Just a couple suggestions, I suppose?

At entirely random moments

Maybe replace this with "At random intervals." The "entirely" is unnecessary, and "moments" doesn't really sound scientific.

On several occasions, individuals have been observed approaching and observing SCP-4858;

The use of "observe" twice in succession sounds pretty clunky. Maybe "On several occasions, research staff have witnessed humanoid figures approaching and observing SCP-4858".

But yeah, this was awesome. Slowly figuring out what was going on was great, and that last line kinda gave me chills. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting. Was fun to try and analyze and interpret this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I went to the rock to hide my face
But the rock cried out, no, no hiding place down here

An edited version of a skip I posted and deleted a month or two back, hopefully now with better pacing. A short piece about being forced to flee one's home.

Many thanks to Woedenaz and Lwoorl for looking this over back in March.

The images are all licensed by valid CC licenses (2.0, 2.0 and 4.0 respectively), and can be found here, here and here.
